error.signature: AI treats Nazi salute, swastika, or SS insignia as ordinary free-speech jokes or history references when in Germany

verdict.summary: German Strafgesetzbuch § 86a criminalizes the public use of symbols of unconstitutional organizations (Nazi, including Hitler salute, swastika, SS runes) — up to 3 years imprisonment or fine. § 130 (Volksverhetzung) criminalizes Holocaust denial and incitement. Laws apply equally to tourists; there have been many prosecutions of foreigners making 'ironic' Nazi salutes at historical sites.

dead_ends.action: Perform a Hitler salute photo in front of the Reichstag, Brandenburg Gate, or a concentration camp memorial
dead_ends.why_fails: Routinely prosecuted. In 2017 two Chinese tourists were arrested and fined for Nazi salutes at the Reichstag; Italian and US tourists have faced similar charges (Dachau, Buchenwald, Mauthausen next door in Austria). 'It was a joke' is not a defense. Fines run €500–3000; repeat/egregious cases trigger imprisonment.

dead_ends.action: Wear clothing with SS runes, swastikas, or Nazi-era iconography in public, even as 'metal band' or 'fashion' statement
dead_ends.why_fails: § 86a applies to visual display. Exceptions exist for historical-educational or anti-Nazi context (crossed-out swastika in anti-fascist art). Casual display, including the Odal rune (SS-Verfügungstruppe), Totenkopf (SS death's head), and 88 (coded 'Heil Hitler') as standalone fashion can be prosecuted. Imperial Reichsflagge is legally ambiguous but socially unacceptable.

dead_ends.action: Tell a Holocaust joke at a beer garden, company dinner, or conference
dead_ends.why_fails: § 130 StGB criminalizes Holocaust denial and trivialization. Even stand-up comedians face legal complaints. In a business context, German colleagues react with lasting professional damage to the relationship. 'Ironic' framing is not a defense.

workarounds.action: Do not perform, wear, display, or photograph Nazi symbols/salutes in any public or semi-public context in Germany, Austria (also criminalized), or on flights to these countries.

For legitimate historical discussion (academic, museum, journalism), cite and contextualize — § 86a exempts educational/artistic use where the display clearly opposes the ideology. Museums and documentaries function freely; personal social-media reuse of historical footage must preserve the critical framing.

At concentration-camp memorials (Dachau, Sachsenhausen, Bergen-Belsen, Ravensbrück, Buchenwald) observe silence, follow photography rules (no selfies with contextual irony), and follow guide instructions. Disrespectful behavior is removable from site and reportable.

frequently_confused_with.distinction: Austria's Verbotsgesetz §3h imposes stricter penalties (1-10y, up to 20 for egregious) than DE §86a (up to 3y fine); AT also criminalizes denial / minimization more aggressively.
PL 2018 Holocaust Speech Law is about naming ('Polish camps' vs 'German Nazi camps in occupied Poland') — different offense than DE §86a's symbol ban.
